lineitem.notes: Largest table, scanned in all queries. Date columns (l_shipdate, l_commitdate, l_receiptdate) are prime candidates for zone maps. Low-cardinality flags (l_returnflag, l_linestatus) benefit from dictionary encoding.

orders.notes: Filtered by date range in Q3. PK-FK join point between customer and lineitem.

customer.notes: Highly selective filter on c_mktsegment (1/5 segments ≈ 20% selectivity). Should be filtered early in Q3 join ordering.

nation.notes: Not accessed in this query subset but present in schema. Very small dimension table.

region.notes: Not accessed in this query subset. Smallest table in schema.

supplier.notes: Not accessed in this query subset.

part.notes: Not accessed in this query subset.

partsupp.notes: Many-to-many bridge table. Not accessed in this query subset.

joins.notes: Q3: Customer is highly selective (20% via mktsegment filter), reduces orders by ~5x. Should be first join after filters.
Q3: After customer→orders filter, this join expands by ~4x (avg 4 lineitems per order). Both sides have date filters that should be pushed down before join.

filters.notes: Date filter: <= 1998-09-02 (approximately 96.5% of data passes). Zone maps highly effective on sorted date column.
Equality filter on low-cardinality string (5 segments). Dictionary encoding enables integer comparison. Push down before joins.
Date filter: < 1995-03-15 (filters ~60% of orders). Zone maps + sorted storage highly beneficial.
Date filter: > 1995-03-15 (filters ~40% of lineitems). Complementary to orders filter - opportunity for correlated filter pushdown.
1-year range out of ~7 years. Perfect for zone map pruning on sorted/clustered data.
Narrow range (0.05 to 0.07) out of ~11 values (0.00-0.10). Can use zone maps on decimal column.
< 24 out of typical range 1-50. Zone maps on integer column.

aggregations.notes: Very low cardinality GROUP BY (l_returnflag ∈ {A,N,R}, l_linestatus ∈ {O,F}). Perfect hash aggregation optimal. Aggregate state: ~80 bytes × 9 groups = 720 bytes (fits in L1 cache).
Medium cardinality (one group per qualifying order). Hash aggregation with pre-sized table (estimate from filtered orders). State: ~40 bytes × 50K groups = 2MB (fits in L3).
Scalar aggregation (no GROUP BY). Single accumulator, no hash table needed. Trivial aggregation.

ordering.notes: Sort on GROUP BY keys (6-9 rows). Negligible cost, can use in-place sort or emit in sorted order if using sorted aggregation.
Top-K optimization opportunity: use min-heap (size 10) during aggregation instead of full sort. Heap maintains top 10 with O(n log k) instead of O(n log n) sort.

optimization_opportunities: Zone maps on date columns (l_shipdate, o_orderdate, l_commitdate, l_receiptdate): Can skip 80-90% of blocks in all queries with date filters (Q1, Q3, Q6)
Dictionary encoding for low-cardinality strings (l_returnflag, l_linestatus, c_mktsegment, o_orderstatus): Enables integer comparisons and reduces memory footprint by 5-10x
Q1 perfect hash aggregation: Only 6-9 groups, use direct array indexing (no hashing) with cache-resident state (~720 bytes)
Q3 join ordering: Apply filters first (customer.c_mktsegment=BUILDING → ~30K customers), then join to orders (~300K orders), then to lineitem. Reduces intermediate result from billions to millions.
Q3 filter pushdown: Date filters (o_orderdate < '1995-03-15' AND l_shipdate > '1995-03-15') should be applied BEFORE joins, reducing input sizes by ~60%
Q3 top-K optimization: Use min-heap of size 10 instead of sorting all groups. Saves 99.9% of sort work.
Q6 conjunctive filter optimization: Apply most selective filters first (l_discount BETWEEN → 20%, then l_quantity < 24 → 48%, then date range → 14%). Combined selectivity ≈ 1.3%.
Q6 SIMD vectorization: Single-table scan with arithmetic predicates is ideal for SIMD. Process 4-8 rows per instruction using AVX2.
Columnar storage for lineitem: All queries select only 5-8 columns out of 16. Column-oriented layout reduces I/O by 50-70%.
Late materialization: For Q3, don't fetch o_orderdate and o_shippriority until after aggregation (only 10 rows). Reduces memory bandwidth.
Operator fusion: Q1 and Q6 can fuse scan+filter+aggregate into single tight loop, avoiding intermediate materialization
Partial aggregation for Q3: Pre-aggregate within lineitem partitions before joining to reduce cross-partition data movement in parallel execution

Correlated filter exploitation (Q3): o_orderdate < '1995-03-15' AND l_shipdate > '1995-03-15' are on opposite sides of the date, creating temporal separation. Possible to use this correlation for smarter join algorithms.
Bloom filter for semi-join reduction (Q3): Build bloom filter on filtered customer keys, probe during orders scan to skip non-matching orders before hash join
String interning: c_mktsegment has only 5 distinct values - intern strings and use pointer equality for O(1) comparison instead of strcmp

query_complexity_ranking.reason: 3-way join with filters on all tables, aggregation, and top-K ordering. Most likely bottleneck. Intermediate results can explode without proper filter pushdown.
Single large table scan, but highly parallel. Multiple aggregations but only 6-9 groups (trivial hash table). Likely dominated by scan I/O.
Single-table scan with scalar aggregation. Simplest query. Likely finishes in <100ms at SF=1 with proper vectorization and filtering.

column_coacccess_patterns.optimization: Store adjacent in columnar layout or as single composite column (9 unique combinations) with dictionary encoding
Always accessed together for revenue computation (l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount)). Consider pre-computing or storing adjacent for cache locality.
Store consecutively in columnar format to fetch in single cache line (3×8 bytes = 24 bytes)
Co-locate for filter evaluation - all three needed to determine tuple qualification
Store together since they're always projected in Q3 result

critical_path_analysis.estimated_intermediate_size_without_opti: 150K customers × 1.5M orders = 225B customer-order pairs (catastrophic if not filtered)
critical_path_analysis.estimated_intermediate_size_with_optimiz: 30K customers × 600K orders (after date filter) × 0.2 selectivity = 3.6M customer-order pairs, then × 4 lineitems = 14.4M final tuples
recommendations: CRITICAL: Apply c_mktsegment and o_orderdate filters BEFORE joins to reduce customer and orders tables by 5x and 2.5x respectively
Join order: filtered_customer ⋈ filtered_orders ⋈ filtered_lineitem (dimension → fact)
Use hash join with customer as build side (smallest after filter)
Partition lineitem by l_orderkey for parallel join if memory permits
Pre-aggregate revenue per orderkey before final top-10 selection

data_distribution_insights.lineitem_dominance: lineitem is 6M rows (75% of total dataset volume). Optimizing lineitem scans is paramount.
data_distribution_insights.date_clustering: Date columns span ~7 years (1992-1998 in TPC-H). If stored sorted by date, zone maps achieve 95%+ block pruning.
data_distribution_insights.join_fanout: orders → lineitem has ~4x fanout (avg 4 lineitems per order). This is moderate and manageable.
data_distribution_insights.skew_potential: c_mktsegment and l_returnflag/l_linestatus are uniform, but real-world data may have skew. Monitor for hotspots in parallel aggregation.
data_distribution_insights.selectivity_composition: Q6 has 3 range filters with combined selectivity ~1.3% (0.14 × 0.20 × 0.48). Applies to 80K out of 6M rows. Perfect for early pruning.

summary: This TPC-H workload subset is analytically diverse: Q6 is a simple filtered scan, Q1 is a scan-aggregate with low-cardinality groups, and Q3 is a multi-way join with aggregation and top-K. Key optimization themes: (1) Zone maps on date columns for 80-90% block pruning, (2) Filter pushdown and optimal join ordering for Q3 to avoid intermediate explosion, (3) Dictionary encoding for low-cardinality strings, (4) Columnar storage to reduce I/O by 50-70%, (5) Perfect hash aggregation for Q1's 6-9 groups, (6) Top-K heap for Q3 instead of full sort, and (7) SIMD vectorization for Q6's arithmetic filters. Q3 is the clear bottleneck and requires careful optimization. With these techniques, expect 10-100x speedup over naive row-based execution.
